planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, average to poor soil in partial to full shade in spring or early fall; stinking iris tolerates dry shade under mature trees better than almost any other flowering perennial and is remarkably adaptable to poor, dry conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in partial to full shade; stinking iris (Gladwin iris, gladdon) is one of the most versatile and underappreciated evergreen perennials for dry, deep shade — its leathery, dark-green, sword-shaped leaves provide attractive year-round foliage structure in conditions where little else thrives, including deep dry shade under dense trees. The small, lilac to pale-mauve flowers in June are modest rather than spectacular, but the true ornamental event comes in fall and winter, when the fat seed pods split open to reveal brilliant rows of hard, glistening, orange-scarlet seeds that persist for months, providing one of the finest berry-like winter displays available in the shade garden. The common name refers to the mildly unpleasant smell of crushed leaves. 'Variegata' (cream-striped leaves) is an ornamental cultivar for brighter effect. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. An essential plant for solving dry, deep-shade problem spots.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant small divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in early fall; blue corydalis goes dormant in summer and benefits from companions that fill in the gap — site with hostas or ferns that expand as corydalis fades.

directions: Plant small divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; blue corydalis is a Chinese native considered by many plantspeople to be the finest and most desirable small shade perennial introduced in the last thirty years — its ferny, deeply divided, blue-green leaves emerge in late winter and spring bearing a prolonged display of slender, tubular, vivid sky-blue to turquoise flowers from April through June. The flower color is uniquely intense and clear — a pure, vibrant blue-turquoise that is extraordinarily rare in shade garden perennials. The plant goes fully dormant in summer (essentially disappears after June heat arrives) and can be interplanted with summer-expanding companions to fill the void. Hardy to zone 5 but struggles in persistent summer heat and humidity in the Deep South. 'Blue Panda', 'China Blue', and 'Pere David' are celebrated cultivars. An absolutely must-have shade garden plant for cool to moderate climate gardeners.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant small divisions in early fall in consistently moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich, sphagnum-based soil in full shade; goldthread requires cool, consistently moist, strongly acidic conditions (pH 4.0–5.0) and is best suited to naturalized, undisturbed woodland areas under conifers in northern gardens.

directions: Plant small divisions in consistently moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade under conifers; goldthread (canker root) is a tiny native evergreen perennial of cool, northern, moist, acidic forests with three ornamental features: glossy, dark-green, three-part leaves with rounded leaflets like miniature strawberry leaves; brilliant yellow, thread-like roots that give the plant its common name; and small white flowers that appear in spring. The plant is extremely small but forms dense, low, evergreen mats in cool, moist, strongly acidic sphagnum-rich forest conditions. The intensely bitter-tasting, bright-yellow roots have a long history of medicinal use in indigenous traditions and early American herbalism as a treatment for mouth sores and canker sores (hence 'canker root'). The alkaloid berberine gives the roots their characteristic yellow color and bitter taste. Requires strictly cool, northern growing conditions — not suited to gardens south of zone 6.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; giant scabious requires considerable space for its full development — allow at least 4 feet on all sides for a mature, well-branched clump.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; giant scabious is a bold, architectural Caucasian perennial that grows to an impressive 6–8 feet in height on wiry, branching, much-divided stems bearing large, deeply lobed, dark-green leaves and a profusion of tall-stemmed, pale primrose-yellow, pincushion-type flowers (similar to but much larger than ornamental Scabiosa) from June through August — the flowers are particularly attractive to bumblebees and butterflies. The entire plant has an elegant, refined appearance despite its large scale, with the flowers appearing on slender, widely branching wiry stems that allow the border plants behind to remain visible. Excellent as a back-of-border specimen in large herbaceous borders, in naturalistic plantings with grasses and meadow perennials, and in cottage-style gardens. Does not require staking if grown in full sun; partial shade may require support. Long-lived and improves in floral display over years.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3–4 inches apart in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in fall; striped squill naturalizes readily and multiplies by offsets and self-seeding to form dense colonies under deciduous trees — ideal for underplanting in drifts of 25 or more bulbs for significant effect.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in fall; striped squill (Lebanon squill, Puschkinia) is a small but exquisite spring bulb from the mountains of Turkey and the Caucasus that produces clusters of delicate, icy-white to pale-blue, bell-shaped flowers, each petal distinctly marked with a deeper blue-violet central stripe — an unusual and charming detail that gives the flower a sparkling, clear quality unlike any other small bulb. It blooms in early spring alongside crocuses and early tulips and naturalizes readily by offsets and self-seeding to form expanding colonies under deciduous trees and in lawns, eventually carpeting large areas in cool blue-white bloom. Tolerates partial shade better than many spring bulbs, making it excellent for naturalizing under trees and in the dappled light at the woodland edge. Plant in masses of 50 or more for a significant display. Foliage disappears by summer, making it compatible with overplanting by summer perennials.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
